items.correct: No — it approaches 0 as the limit but no term is ever exactly 0.
items.misconception: Eventually a term will be so small it equals 0.
items.random_wrong: It reaches 0 at the millionth term.
items.why: Students see terms 'getting really small' and assume they reach zero; they don't grasp the idea of approaching a limit without reaching it.

items.q: If the discriminant of a quadratic is negative, what does it tell you about real roots?
items.correct: There are no real roots — the graph does not cross the x-axis.
items.misconception: There is one repeated root — the curve just touches the x-axis.
items.why: Students confuse 'discriminant zero' (one repeated root) with 'discriminant negative' (no real roots) because both feel like 'unusual' cases.

items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.
The answer examiners want is: 5x^4. “x^4” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 1/x. “e^x” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 12. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
